patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the btrfs filesystem by replacing a lock-holding read operation with a lockless one, improving scalability under heavy memory reclaim conditions.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Gave a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 02:23:46PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> From: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>
> 
> Under heavy memcg-driven slab reclaim with many memcgs and CPUs,
> shrink_slab_memcg() invokes the per-superblock count callback once
> per (memcg, NUMA node) tuple. For btrfs that callback reaches
> percpu_counter_sum_positive() on fs_info->evictable_extent_maps,
> which takes the percpu_counter's raw spinlock with IRQs disabled and
> walks every online CPU. With hundreds of memcgs driving reclaim on a
> host with dozens of CPUs, this counter lock becomes a global
> serialization point: profiles show CPU pinned in the spin_lock_irqsave
> acquire under __percpu_counter_sum, with cross-CPU IPIs hitting
> csd_lock_wait_toolong while waiting for spinning vCPUs.
> 
> The shrinker count is advisory -- super_cache_count() already notes
> "counts can change between super_cache_count and super_cache_scan, so
> we really don't need locks here." Use percpu_counter_read_positive(),
> which is lockless. Worst-case skew is bounded by batch * num_online_cpus
> (a few thousand), negligible compared to the millions of extent maps a
> busy filesystem accumulates and well within the noise that the shrinker
> already tolerates.
> 
> Tested-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>

Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs
[PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] nfsd: fix BUG_ON in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid on racing delegation revoke
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server that can cause a BUG_ON when trying to allocate a layout stateid for a delegated file. The issue occurs when a concurrent delegation revoke is triggered, causing the fi_deleg_file pointer to be cleared under the fi_lock lock. To fix this, the patch takes an RCU read lock around the fi_deleg_file read in the SC_TYPE_DELEG path and replaces the BUG_ON with a graceful error return that cleans up any partially initialized layout stateid. This ensures that the NFS server can handle concurrent delegation revokes without crashing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] nfsd: RCU-protect cl_cb_session to fix use-after-free on session teardown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFS daemon by converting the cl_cb_session pointer to an RCU-protected pointer. The bug occurs when a session is destroyed while rpciod still holds an inflight callback task that dereferences the freed session. The patch uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to ensure that readers on rpciod do not access freed memory. It also moves assignments of cl_cb_session to after successful creation of a backchannel and clears it on error returns, and switches from kfree() to kfree_rcu() for freeing the session slab.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] nfsd: convert nfsd_net boolean flags to unsigned long flags word
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the boolean flags in nfsd_net to a single unsigned long flags word, using an enum to define the bit positions. This change fixes a race condition in the grace_ended flag by using test_and_set_bit(), which is atomic on all architectures. The remaining flags are converted to use test_bit/set_bit/clear_bit for consistency and to avoid sub-word cmpxchg issues on certain architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] nfsd: dedup nfs4_client_to_reclaim inserts
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd kernel module where the nfs4_client_to_reclaim function unconditionally allocates new memory for each client, leading to incorrect behavior and potential crashes when the system is rebooted. The fix involves looking up existing clients using nfsd4_find_reclaim_client before allocating new memory, and adding a read-write semaphore to serialize access to the reclaim table. This ensures that the correct records are updated and avoids unnecessary memory allocations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] nfsd: gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFSv3 ACL implementation where set_posix_acl() was being called unconditionally, leading to incorrect behavior when setting or removing ACLs. The fix involves adding checks for the argp->mask bits before calling set_posix_acl(), ensuring that only the relevant ACLs are modified. This change mirrors the existing logic in nfsd3_proc_getacl().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] NFSD: check truncate permission under inode lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a timing issue in the NFS server where a client can extend a file before the server checks for truncation permission. The patch splits the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check, ensuring that the correct size is used when checking for permission. This fix closes a TOCTOU window and prevents concurrent appends from extending the file before the server takes inode lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] nfsd: fix partial-write detection in nfsd_direct_write
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_direct_write function where it incorrectly detects short writes and continues writing to the wrong file offset. The issue arises from the fact that vfs_iocb_iter_write advances the iter by the number of bytes written, making segments[i].iter.count hold the residual length instead of the original request length. To fix this, a snapshot of the segment's byte count is taken before the write and compared against host_err to accurately detect short writes. This ensures that any short write breaks the loop and prevents over-reporting *cnt to the NFS client.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: cap decoded POSIX ACL count to bound sort cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance issue in the NFS server by capping the number of POSIX ACL entries that can be decoded from a client request. The decoder previously allowed an unlimited number of entries, which led to unbounded CPU usage due to an O(n^2) bubble sort operation. The patch introduces a check to reject requests with more than the maximum allowed number of entries (NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES), preventing excessive CPU usage and ensuring that ACLs set via NFSv4 POSIX ACL extensions are retrievable via NFSACL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] nfsd: validate symlink target length in NFSv4 CREATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a security vulnerability in the NFSv4 protocol by validating the length of symbolic link targets to prevent a client from forcing an unbounded allocation of memory.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various bugs and issues in the NFS daemon, including remotely-triggerable panics, use-after-free errors, and denial-of-service vulnerabilities. The patches also include protocol fixes and improvements to the NFSv4 implementation. Specifically, the series includes changes to fix a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page(), validate symlink target lengths, cap decoded POSIX ACL counts, and gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for fetching NFS server statistics using Generic Netlink, a new feature in the kernel. When netlink is available, nfsstat uses it to retrieve statistics from the server. If netlink is not available (e.g., on older kernels), it falls back to parsing /proc/net/rpc/nfsd as before. The patch adds code to send a dump command and parse the reply to populate various stat arrays, including reply cache, filehandle, IO, network, RPC, and per-version procedure call counts.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch introduces a check for svc pool percpu counter allocation, but reviewer Jeff Layton suggests avoiding the hassle of struct randomization and keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns that struct randomization could break the patch and suggested keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls to avoid hassle.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Agreed with Jeff that the patch is 'too clever by half', implying disapproval of its approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/30/26 5:48 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 17:45 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
>> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 16:21 -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
>>> From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>>
>>> __svc_create() initializes three per-pool percpu_counter stats and
>>> ignores every return value. On SMP, percpu_counter_init() fails when
>>> __alloc_percpu_gfp() cannot satisfy the allocation, leaving the failed
>>> counter with fbc->counters == NULL and its embedded raw_spinlock_t,
>>> list_head, and count never initialized. __svc_create() returns the
>>> half-constructed svc_serv to nfsd, lockd, or the NFS callback service
>>> anyway.
>>>
>>> Once that service is live, the hot-path increments in
>>> svc_xprt_enqueue(), svc_handle_xprt(), and
>>> svc_pool_wake_idle_thread() reach a counter whose backing pointer is
>>> NULL. The pointer is a per-cpu offset, so the access does not fault:
>>> it resolves to offset zero of the current CPU's per-cpu area and
>>> silently corrupts whatever variable lives there. A
>>> /proc/fs/nfsd/pool_stats read walks the same NULL per-cpu storage and
>>> returns garbage, and on CONFIG_DEBUG_SPINLOCK or lockdep it splats on
>>> the never-initialized lock.
>>>
>>> Creating the broken service requires a percpu allocation failure during
>>> RPC server startup, so it is reachable only by a local administrator
>>> under memory pressure or fault injection; a remote peer cannot induce
>>> the bad state on its own.
>>>
>>> Initialize the three adjacent pool counters with one checked
>>> percpu_counter_init_many() call and fail __svc_create() when the
>>> allocation fails, unwinding the counters for pools already set up. Use
>>> the matching percpu_counter_destroy_many() at teardown so the single
>>> per-cpu allocation is freed exactly once.
>>>
>>> Fixes: ccf08bed6e7a ("SUNRPC: Replace pool stats with per-CPU variables")
>>> Signed-off-by: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>> ---
>>>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 32 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
>>>  1 file changed, 26 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
>>>
>>> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> index ae9ec4bf34f7..aeb6e631848c 100644
>>> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> @@ -476,6 +476,22 @@ __svc_init_bc(struct svc_serv *serv)
>>>  }
>>>  #endif
>>>  
>>> +enum {
>>> +	SVC_POOL_COUNTERS = 3,
>>> +};
>>> +
>>> +static int svc_pool_init_counters(struct svc_pool *pool)
>>> +{
>>> +	return percpu_counter_init_many(&pool->sp_messages_arrived, 0,
>>> +					GFP_KERNEL, SVC_POOL_COUNTERS);
>>> +}
>>>
>>>
>>
>> Switching to this looks like a good thing, but it means that the
>> svc_pool struct fields now have some strict ordering requirements. The
>> percpu_counters all need to be snuggled up together.
>>
>> That deserves a comment to that effect in the struct svc_pool, so that
>> we don't inadvertently break it later.
>>
> 
> Actually, sashiko points out that struct randomization could break here
> too. Given that service creation isn't a hot codepath, I'd keep these
> as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls and avoid the hassle.
Yeah. It's too clever by half.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup when there's a race between adding an object to the LRU and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the effective memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function in mm/list_lru.c, which is used to synchronize access to list lru nodes. The patch shares the code between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop by flattening it and reducing nesting levels, making the code more readable and maintainable.

review_comments.author: Dev Jain
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag without any specific technical feedback or suggestions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/2] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory management to bypass the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead mappings. The mmap_miss heuristic is intended to prevent speculative mmap readahead when a file appears to be randomly accessed, but this does not fit well with the VM_EXEC path. The patch changes the behavior of do_async_mmap_readahead and filemap_map_pages to skip both increments and decrements of the mmap_miss counter for VM_EXEC mappings, matching the existing treatment for VM_SEQ_READ mappings. This ensures that the counter remains symmetric and accurate for executable mappings.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is reiterating the original patch description and reasoning, emphasizing that VM_EXEC readahead is already constrained differently from ordinary mmap read-around, and that skipping both increments and decrements for VM_EXEC mappings keeps the counter accounting symmetric.
review_comments.raw_body:
The mmap_miss heuristic is intended to stop speculative mmap readahead
when a file looks like a random-access workload. That does not fit the
VM_EXEC path very well.

VM_EXEC readahead is already constrained differently from ordinary mmap
read-around: it is bounded by the VMA, uses exec_folio_order() to choose
an order useful for executable mappings, and sets async_size to 0 so it
does not create follow-on readahead. When VM_HUGEPAGE is also present,
the larger readahead is an explicit userspace opt-in.

The mmap_miss counter is decremented from cache-hit paths in
do_async_mmap_readahead() and filemap_map_pages(). Those paths are not
always enough to balance the synchronous miss increments for executable
mappings. In particular, when fault-around is effectively disabled, such
as configurations where fault_around_pages is 1, filemap_map_pages() is
not reached from the fault path. The counter can then become a stale
throttle for VM_EXEC mappings and suppress the readahead behavior that
the executable-specific path is trying to provide.

Skip both mmap_miss increments and decrements for VM_EXEC mappings,
matching the existing VM_SEQ_READ treatment and keeping the counter
accounting symmetric.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
Reviewed-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
---
 mm/filemap.c | 14 +++++++-------
 1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
index cca20e350c95..a16b33e0fc71 100644
--- a/mm/filemap.c
+++ b/mm/filemap.c
@@ -3339,7 +3339,7 @@ static struct file *do_sync_mmap_readahead(struct vm_fault *vmf)
 		}
 	}
 
-	if (!(vm_flags & VM_SEQ_READ)) {
+	if (!(vm_flags & (VM_SEQ_READ | VM_EXEC))) {
 		/* Avoid banging the cache line if not needed */
 		mmap_miss = READ_ONCE(ra->mmap_miss);
 		if (mmap_miss < MMAP_LOTSAMISS * 10)
@@ -3434,12 +3434,12 @@ static struct file *do_async_mmap_readahead(struct vm_fault *vmf,
 	 * times for a single folio and break the balance with mmap_miss
 	 * increase in do_sync_mmap_readahead().
 	 *
-	 * VM_SEQ_READ mappings skip the mmap_miss increment in
+	 * VM_SEQ_READ and VM_EXEC mappings skip the mmap_miss increment in
 	 * do_sync_mmap_readahead(), so skip the decrement here as well to
 	 * keep the counter symmetric.
 	 */
 	if (likely(!folio_test_locked(folio)) &&
-	    !(vmf->vma->vm_flags & VM_SEQ_READ)) {
+	    !(vmf->vma->vm_flags & (VM_SEQ_READ | VM_EXEC))) {
 		mmap_miss = READ_ONCE(ra->mmap_miss);
 		if (mmap_miss)
 			WRITE_ONCE(ra->mmap_miss, --mmap_miss);
@@ -3941,14 +3941,14 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
 		 * Don't decrease mmap_miss in this scenario to make sure
 		 * we can stop read-ahead.
 		 *
-		 * VM_SEQ_READ mappings skip the mmap_miss increment in
-		 * do_sync_mmap_readahead(), so skip the decrement here as
-		 * well to keep the counter symmetric.
+		 * VM_SEQ_READ and VM_EXEC mappings skip the mmap_miss
+		 * increment in do_sync_mmap_readahead(), so skip the
+		 * decrement here as well to keep the counter symmetric.
 		 */
 		if ((map_ret & VM_FAULT_NOPAGE) &&
 		    !(vmf->flags & FAULT_FLAG_TRIED) &&
 		    !folio_test_workingset(folio) &&
-		    !(vma->vm_flags & VM_SEQ_READ)) {
+		    !(vma->vm_flags & (VM_SEQ_READ | VM_EXEC))) {
 			unsigned short mmap_miss;
 
 			mmap_miss = READ_ONCE(file->f_ra.mmap_miss);
-- 
2.52.0

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/2] mm: use mapping_max_folio_order() for force_thp_readahead order
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() to use mapping_max_folio_order() when HPAGE_PMD_ORDER exceeds MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER. This allows VM_HUGEPAGE mappings on certain configurations, such as arm64 with a 64K base page size, to request large folios instead of falling back to non-forced mmap readahead. The patch introduces a new cap of 2MB for the allocation order, chosen based on its alignment with existing PMD sizes and contiguous-PTE block sizes. This change enables VM_HUGEPAGE mappings to take advantage of large-folio readahead requests, reducing TLB pressure in the process.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() should use mapping_max_folio_order(mapping) when HPAGE_PMD_ORDER exceeds MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER, and agreed to update the code accordingly.

review_comments.author: Pedro Falcato
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Pedro Falcato pointed out that the chosen cap of 2MB for large folio readahead on arm64 is incorrect and should be 32M, which matches the PMD size for a 16K base page size.

reviewer suggested a different logic for determining when to enable forced THP readahead, using min3() instead of arbitrary values like 2MB

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a minor mistake in the commit message, agreed to correct it, and offered an alternative solution of sending a new patch revision.

Author addressed Pedro Falcato's concern that the current code may cause high memory pressure due to large file folios, and explained that the chosen 2MB cap is a compromise between architectures and base page sizes. The author believes the current code maintains existing behavior while providing large folios in a controlled way, but acknowledged that raising the cap later might be difficult.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, should have just said 4K. Messed up when rewriting the commit
message. Andrew Thanks for merging in mm-new, would it be possible
to remove "or 16K" from the commit message. I can send a new revision
as well if that is easier and preferred.

---

So this can result in extreme memory pressure. For example, on 64K base page size,
with HPAGE_PMD_ORDER = 13 (512M) and MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER = 11 (128M), this will evaluate
THP order to 128M. I think Jan mentioned in previous versions that there are already
reports of high memory pressure due to large file folios, and we are seeing this
in our fleet as well with xfs.

I chose 2M cap as that is what most (not all) architectures which currently support this
hugepage code flow are currently used to, and on archs + base page size combination which
just evaluate this code to base page, for example 64K base page size, it will provide a
performance uplift without causing excessive memory pressure.

In pagemap.h, we have

#define PREFERRED_MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER	HPAGE_PMD_ORDER
#define MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER	min(MAX_XAS_ORDER, PREFERRED_MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER)

So MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER <= HPAGE_PMD_ORDER is always true.

At filesystem/inode setup: mapping_set_folio_order_range() 

	if (max > MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER)
		max = MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER;

so mapping_max_folio_order() <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER is also always true.

which means mapping_max_folio_order(mapping) <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER <= HPAGE_PMD_ORDER is always
true, and you dont need the min3(..) in your diff.

Now the question is if then why not just do:

	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE) && (vm_flags & VM_HUGEPAGE)) {
		if (mapping_large_folio_support(mapping)) {
			force_thp_readahead = true;
			thp_order = min_t(unsigned int,
					  mapping_max_folio_order(mapping),
					  get_order(SZ_2M));
		}
	}


This is because this will regress the 16K ARM case where we already got 32M
folios. Someone might upgrade the kernel and start getting 2M folios now.

So IMHO the current code is correct, both in maintaining what the
existing behaviour has been, but also trying to provide large folios to
architectures that currently dont get them but in a controlled way.

Later on, when we think or evaluate that 2M was too small a cap, it can
be raised. I think its very easy to raise a cap, but much more difficult
to make the cap smaller, as we might get reports on kernel upgrades that
someone was getting a certain size of folio but now its much smaller.

As a summary, what my code in this current form gives is:
1) 4K x86/ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=9 <= 9): first branch -> 2M unchanged behaviour
2) 16K ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=11 <= 11): first branch -> 32M unchanged behaviour
3) 64K ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=13 <= 11): new else-if -> 2M new capability, conservative
behaviour, which can be increased later if needed.
I know that 64K base page size having 2M large folios while 16K base page size
having 32M sounds counterintuitive, but I think 32M on 16K was a mistake and
we shouldnt repeat that mistake for 64K.

What your change with min3(mapping_max_folio_order(mapping), MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER, HPAGE_PMD_ORDER)
will give
3) 64K ARM -> 128M folios, extremely high memory pressure.

What only having min(mapping_max_folio_order, get_order(SZ_2M)) will give:
2) 16K ARM -> 2M folios, (regression since we always had 32M on 16K ARM).

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author is addressing a concern about explaining the reasoning behind choosing 2MB as the cap for forced readahead order, and suggests adding a comment to clarify this.

The author is addressing a concern about the lack of explanation for the design decision to cap the forced readahead order at 2MB, and has added a comment in the code to explain this choice.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think might be good to include the below comment to explain the decision being made
here:

---

From: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Date: Fri, 29 May 2026 05:31:05 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] [fixlet] mm/filemap: add comment explaining design decision

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/filemap.c | 8 ++++++++
 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
index bfb891d9da1f..0a3facf452b3 100644
--- a/mm/filemap.c
+++ b/mm/filemap.c
@@ -3319,6 +3319,14 @@ static struct file *do_sync_mmap_readahead(struct vm_fault *vmf)
 
 	/* Use the readahead code, even if readahead is disabled */
 	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE) && (vm_flags & VM_HUGEPAGE)) {
+		/*
+		 * Preserve PMD-sized readahead where it already fits in
+		 * the page cache. Otherwise cap the new fallback path at
+		 * 2MB: this is the common PMD-sized hugepage size, and it
+		 * avoids memory pressure from very large forced readahead
+		 * when mapping_max_folio_order() is high (for example,
+		 * 128MB with 64K base pages on arm64).
+		 */
 		if (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER) {
 			force_thp_readahead = true;
 			thp_order = HPAGE_PMD_ORDER;
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

review_comments.author: Pedro Falcato
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Pedro Falcato noted that memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios and questioned the efficiency of using THPs at non-PMD levels, but acknowledged it's not a major issue in this specific case.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato noted that HPAGE_PMD_ORDER may exceed MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER on arm64 configurations, and suggested that the code should handle this case to avoid potential issues in the future.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato suggested capping the maximum folio order at 32MB instead of 2MB to simplify logic, noting that if an architecture doesn't support 32MiB folios, it will clamp the order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER

reviewer argued that the patch's assumption about large folios being beneficial is flawed, citing reports of workloads regressing due to thrashing and TLB thrashing, and expressed concern that changing the logic will inevitably affect someone
review_comments.raw_body:
For what it's worth, memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios. But
yes, I understand.

For what it's worth, I don't think it's such a big issue in this case - you're
asking for THPs, and you're getting them - though maybe not efficiently because
these aren't at the PMD level. But this is also true for e.g x86 where an
an order-3 folio or order-7 folio are completely useless for the hardware, since
there is no mTHP to speak of (and, with that, you also lose PG_active and
PG_reference precision as you only keep one of each for each folio, amongst
other tradeoffs).

---

Right, good point. (well, I suppose it will not be true in the future as
someone tries to harness PMD-level cont bits)

---

So maybe limit to 32MB? It's still arbitrary but at least you get simpler
logic. If the architecture does not support 32MiB folios, it will clamp
the maximum folio order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER, and you get the same result.

Does this sound correct?

---

I don't think this is a valid argument. You can also have reports that now
folios are too large and they're regressing workloads because of thrashing/
compaction/possibly TLB thrashing. And indeed we have several reports of that
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260403193535.9970-1-dipiets@amazon.it/
comes to mind, plus all of the anon THP regressions over the years, including
in $current_year).

Bottom line is that changing things will always affect someone :) Particularly
since the logic we have is not too careful at deciding what should or should
not be a THP (both in anon and file cases). And if (once?) we make it smarter,
it will surely also regress someone!

-- 
Pedro

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to a concern about the patch's impact on AMD CPUs by explaining that hardware TLB coalescing is beneficial at 32kB and would reduce page faults.

Author agrees that replacing SZ_2M with a larger value like SZ_32M sounds correct but expresses concern about the size being too large.

Author acknowledges that there are performance issues on x86 systems with large xfs folios, but does not provide a clear resolution or plan for addressing these concerns.

Author agrees with reviewer's suggestion to keep the cap at 2M initially and asks for feedback from other reviewers.
sentiment_signals: does not provide a clear resolution
review_comments.raw_body:
So there is hardware TLB coalescing on almost every new AMD CPU, I believe it is
at 32kB, and we would benefit from that. On top of that, we would reduce pagefaults.

---

Yes, so if we replace it with SZ_32M, it sounds correct. I just think
the 32M size is too large. But as you pointed out, even 2M can be too large...

---

Ack, and yes we have seen both thrashing and compaction issues due to xfs
large folios in the fleet with x86..

---

Yes completely agree on this as well.

So personally I do have a preference of keeping the cap at 2M atleast initially
while we currently try and solve the issues we see with 2M alone. As we are already
seeing reports of thrashing and compaction with just 2M, I dont think the logic
in this patch with just an if else is that complicated.

Matthew, Jan, do you have any thoughts or strong preferences on cap size?

Thanks!

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Jan Kara suggested an alternative option (limiting at 2MB with simple logic) and questioned the practical performance impact of larger folios on 16k pagesize ARM, asking if it's significant enough to warrant the change.

Reviewer Jan Kara suggested trying option 3 for simplicity and to gauge user feedback before deciding between options 1 and 2.
review_comments.raw_body:
So AFAIU the practical discussion is about two options:

1) limiting at 2MB with a slighly more complicated logic to keep mapping at
PMD order for 16k pagesize on ARM but use 2MB pages for 64k pagesize on ARM

or

2) limit at 32MB with simple logic which results in larger (32MB) folios
with 16k and 64k pagesize on ARM and thus larger memory overhead.

I'd like to maybe offer option 3): limit at 2MB with simple logic. This
will reduce folio size on 16k pagesize ARM compared to 1) but do we really
care? I.e., is there big enough practical performance impact with conpte
and other tricks ARM is playing?

---

Frankly, no strong opinion. I'd think 3) is worth trying for its simplicity
and seeing whether somebody complains, otherwise I can live with both 1)
and 2).

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch improves large folio readahead for exec memory by addressing two issues in the do_sync_mmap_readahead() function. The first issue is that the mmap_miss heuristic, which is meant to throttle speculative readahead, is also applied to the VM_EXEC readahead path, causing it to be disabled prematurely on certain configurations. The second issue is that the force_thp_readahead path is gated only on HPAGE_PMD_ORDER being less than or equal to MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER, preventing large folios from being used in some cases. The patches address these issues by making the mmap_miss heuristic symmetric for VM_SEQ_READ and generalizing the gate for force_thp_readahead so that any mapping advertising a usefully large maximum folio order can benefit.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a TOCTOU data race issue pointed out by sashiko, agreed to avoid making it worse by introducing the thp_order local variable.
review_comments.raw_body:
Two checks in do_sync_mmap_readahead() limit large-folio readahead:

  1. The mmap_miss heuristic is meant to throttle wasteful speculative
     readahead. It is currently also applied to the VM_EXEC readahead
     path, which is targeted rather than speculative. Once mmap_miss exceeds
     MMAP_LOTSAMISS, exec readahead - including the large-folio
     order requested by exec_folio_order() - is disabled. On
     configurations where the mmap_miss decrement paths are not
     active (see patch 1) the counter only grows, so exec readahead
     is permanently disabled after the first 100 faults.

  2. The force_thp_readahead path is gated only on
     HPAGE_PMD_ORDER <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER and always drives the
     readahead at HPAGE_PMD_ORDER. Configurations where
     HPAGE_PMD_ORDER exceeds MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER never reach this
     path, even when the mapping itself supports usefully large
     folios well below the cap.

Both issues are most visible on arm64 with a 64K base page size,
where HPAGE_PMD_ORDER is 13 (512MB) -- above MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER
(11) -- and where fault_around_pages collapses to 1 disabling
should_fault_around() (one of the two mmap_miss decrement sites).
However the fixes are architecture-agnostic: patch 1 reflects the
nature of VM_EXEC readahead regardless of base page size, and
patch 2 generalises the gate so any mapping advertising a usefully
large maximum folio order can benefit.

I created a benchmark that mmaps a large executable file madvises it
as huge and calls RET-stub functions at PAGE_SIZE offsets across it.
"Cold" measures fault + readahead cost. "Random" first faults in all
pages with a sequential sweep (not measured), then measures time for
calling random offsets, isolating iTLB miss cost for scattered execution.

The benchmark results on Neoverse V2 (Grace), arm64 with 64K base pages,
512MB executable file on ext4, averaged over 3 runs:

  Phase      | Baseline     | Patched      | Improvement
  -----------|--------------|--------------|------------------
  Cold fault | 83.4 ms      | 41.3 ms      | 50% faster
  Random     | 76.0 ms      | 58.3 ms      | 23% faster

The patches are on top of mm-unstable from 28 May
(8a74e22643189e0ae339afc91110ddb4cab1941b) which include patch [1]
that make mmap_miss accounting symmetric for VM_SEQ_READ which was pointed
out by sashiko in the previous revision.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260525145751.2671248-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 

Kiryl and Jan, I have kept your Reviewed-by tags from the previous revision
for patch 1 as the concept is still the same, please let me know if that
is not ok.

v5 -> v6: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260522162422.3856502-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/
- Based on top of patch [1] (sashiko)
- Changes to commit message to make it more accurate for patch 1 and skip
  mmap_miss decrement as well. (sashiko)
- Keep old behaviour if large folio mappings is not enabled (sashiko).
- sashiko pointed to a TOCTOU data race that was pre-existing. My patch
  could make it worse. Dont make it worse by introducing thp_order local
  variable.

v3 -> v5: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260402181326.3107102-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/
- (Looks like I messed up the versioning here and went directly form
  v3 to v5.)
- Drop patches for elf thp unmapped area alignment and deal with them
  separately. These patches will just bring folios smaller than PMD
  at the same level as PMD. The 2 patches now should be much easier
  to merge.
- Tackle size of THP for exec pages at the same point as PMD instead
  of tackling using exec_folio_order() (Ryan during LSFMM, Thanks!)

v2 -> v3: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260320140315.979307-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/
- Take into account READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS for elf alignment by aligning
  to HPAGE_PMD_SIZE limited to 2M (Rui)
- Reviewed-by tags for patch 1 from Kiryl and Jan
- Remove preferred_exec_order() (Jan)
- Change ra->order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER if vma_pages(vma) >= HPAGE_PMD_NR
  otherwise use exec_folio_order() with gfp &= ~__GFP_RECLAIM for
  do_sync_mmap_readahead().
- Change exec_folio_order() to return 2M (cont-pte size) for 64K base
  page size for arm64.
- remove bprm->file NULL check (Matthew)
- Change filp to file (Matthew)
- Improve checking of p_vaddr and p_vaddr (Rui and Matthew)

v1 -> v2: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260310145406.3073394-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/
- disable mmap_miss logic for VM_EXEC (Jan Kara)
- Align in elf only when segment VA and file offset are already aligned (Rui)
- preferred_exec_order() for VM_EXEC sync mmap_readahead which takes into
  account zone high watermarks (as an approximation of memory pressure)
  (David, or atleast my approach to what David suggested in [1] :))
- Extend max alignment to mapping_max_folio_size() instead of
  exec_folio_order()
 
Usama Arif (2):
  mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
  mm: use mapping_max_folio_order() for force_thp_readahead order

 mm/filemap.c | 41 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++---------------
 1 file changed, 26 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)

--

1) 4K x86/ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=9 <= 9): first branch -> 2M unchanged behaviour
2) 16K ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=11 <= 11): first branch -> 32M unchanged behaviour
3) 64K ARM (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=13 <= 11): new else-if -> 2M new capability, conservative
behaviour, which can be increased later if needed.
I know that 64K base page size having 2M large folios while 16K base page size
having 32M sounds counterintuitive, but I think 32M on 16K was a mistake and
we shouldnt repeat that mistake for 64K.

What your change with min3(mapping_max_folio_order(mapping), MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER, HPAGE_PMD_ORDER)
will give
3) 64K ARM -> 128M folios, extremely high memory pressure.

What only having min(mapping_max_folio_order, get_order(SZ_2M)) will give:
2) 16K ARM -> 2M folios, (regression since we always had 32M on 16K ARM).

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author is addressing a concern about the need for additional comments in the patch, specifically explaining the decision behind disabling exec readahead when mmap_miss exceeds MMAP_LOTSAMISS.

The author is addressing a concern about the design decision behind preserving PMD-sized readahead in the filemap code, specifically when mapping_max_folio_order() is high. They added a comment to explain this decision and preserved the existing behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think might be good to include the below comment to explain the decision being made
here:

---

From: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Date: Fri, 29 May 2026 05:31:05 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] [fixlet] mm/filemap: add comment explaining design decision

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/filemap.c | 8 ++++++++
 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
index bfb891d9da1f..0a3facf452b3 100644
--- a/mm/filemap.c
+++ b/mm/filemap.c
@@ -3319,6 +3319,14 @@ static struct file *do_sync_mmap_readahead(struct vm_fault *vmf)
 
 	/* Use the readahead code, even if readahead is disabled */
 	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE) && (vm_flags & VM_HUGEPAGE)) {
+		/*
+		 * Preserve PMD-sized readahead where it already fits in
+		 * the page cache. Otherwise cap the new fallback path at
+		 * 2MB: this is the common PMD-sized hugepage size, and it
+		 * avoids memory pressure from very large forced readahead
+		 * when mapping_max_folio_order() is high (for example,
+		 * 128MB with 64K base pages on arm64).
+		 */
 		if (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER) {
 			force_thp_readahead = true;
 			thp_order = HPAGE_PMD_ORDER;
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

review_comments.author: Pedro Falcato
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer notes that memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios and suggests that the issue may not be as significant in this case because THPs are being requested, but not efficiently used due to their size.

The reviewer notes that the current implementation of large-folio readahead for exec memory may not hold up in the future due to potential changes in page table management, specifically the use of PMD-level cont bits.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato suggested limiting the large-folio readahead to 32MB instead of an arbitrary value, which would simplify logic and ensure that if a system doesn't support 32MiB folios, it will clamp the maximum folio order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato expressed concerns that the proposed changes to large folio readahead may have unintended consequences, such as regressing workloads due to thrashing or TLB thrashing, and pointed out existing reports of similar issues with anonymous THP regressions.
review_comments.raw_body:
For what it's worth, memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios. But
yes, I understand.

For what it's worth, I don't think it's such a big issue in this case - you're
asking for THPs, and you're getting them - though maybe not efficiently because
these aren't at the PMD level. But this is also true for e.g x86 where an
an order-3 folio or order-7 folio are completely useless for the hardware, since
there is no mTHP to speak of (and, with that, you also lose PG_active and
PG_reference precision as you only keep one of each for each folio, amongst
other tradeoffs).

---

Right, good point. (well, I suppose it will not be true in the future as
someone tries to harness PMD-level cont bits)

---

So maybe limit to 32MB? It's still arbitrary but at least you get simpler
logic. If the architecture does not support 32MiB folios, it will clamp
the maximum folio order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER, and you get the same result.

Does this sound correct?

---

I don't think this is a valid argument. You can also have reports that now
folios are too large and they're regressing workloads because of thrashing/
compaction/possibly TLB thrashing. And indeed we have several reports of that
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260403193535.9970-1-dipiets@amazon.it/
comes to mind, plus all of the anon THP regressions over the years, including
in $current_year).

Bottom line is that changing things will always affect someone :) Particularly
since the logic we have is not too careful at deciding what should or should
not be a THP (both in anon and file cases). And if (once?) we make it smarter,
it will surely also regress someone!

-- 
Pedro

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged the potential benefits of hardware TLB coalescing on AMD CPUs, specifically at a 32kB threshold, and noted it could lead to reduced page faults.

Author agrees that the suggested replacement for SZ_4M is correct but expresses concern about the chosen value of 32M being too large.

Author acknowledges that large folio readahead issues exist on x86 systems, confirming a problem similar to arm64.

Author agrees with Pedro's feedback and suggests keeping the cap at 2M initially to avoid thrashing and compaction issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
So there is hardware TLB coalescing on almost every new AMD CPU, I believe it is
at 32kB, and we would benefit from that. On top of that, we would reduce pagefaults.

---

Yes, so if we replace it with SZ_32M, it sounds correct. I just think
the 32M size is too large. But as you pointed out, even 2M can be too large...

---

Ack, and yes we have seen both thrashing and compaction issues due to xfs
large folios in the fleet with x86..

---

Yes completely agree on this as well.

So personally I do have a preference of keeping the cap at 2M atleast initially
while we currently try and solve the issues we see with 2M alone. As we are already
seeing reports of thrashing and compaction with just 2M, I dont think the logic
in this patch with just an if else is that complicated.

Matthew, Jan, do you have any thoughts or strong preferences on cap size?

Thanks!

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Jan Kara suggested an alternative option (3) to limit large folio readahead at 2MB, which would reduce memory overhead on 16k pagesize ARM compared to the original options, and questioned whether a significant performance impact exists.

Reviewer Jan Kara suggested trying option 3 as a simple alternative to options 1 and 2, but expressed no strong opinion on the matter.
review_comments.raw_body:
So AFAIU the practical discussion is about two options:

1) limiting at 2MB with a slighly more complicated logic to keep mapping at
PMD order for 16k pagesize on ARM but use 2MB pages for 64k pagesize on ARM

or

2) limit at 32MB with simple logic which results in larger (32MB) folios
with 16k and 64k pagesize on ARM and thus larger memory overhead.

I'd like to maybe offer option 3): limit at 2MB with simple logic. This
will reduce folio size on 16k pagesize ARM compared to 1) but do we really
care? I.e., is there big enough practical performance impact with conpte
and other tricks ARM is playing?

---

Frankly, no strong opinion. I'd think 3) is worth trying for its simplicity
and seeing whether somebody complains, otherwise I can live with both 1)
and 2).
